{{short description|Education occupation of guidance and counseling}}
{{Educational research}}{{Worldwide|date=July 2024|2=US|section|reason=}}
A '''school counselor''' is a certified/licensed professional that provides academic, career, college readiness, and social-emotional support for all students. There are school counselor positions within each level of schooling (elementary, middle, high, and college). By developing and following a school counseling program, school counselors are able to provide students of all ages with the appropriate support and guidance needed for overall success.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Home - American School Counselor Association (ASCA) |url=https://schoolcounselor.org/ |access-date=2023-06-23 |website=schoolcounselor.org}}</ref>

==Purpose==
Professional school counselors ideally implement a school counseling program that promotes and enhances student achievement.<ref name= "Hatch"/> A framework for appropriate and inappropriate school counselor responsibilities and roles is outlined in the ASCA National Model.<ref name= "Hatch"/>

School counselors are employed in elementary, middle, and high schools, in district supervisory settings, in Counselor Education faculty positions (usually with an earned Ph.D. in Counselor Education in the USA or related graduate doctorates abroad), and post-secondary settings doing academic, career, college access/affordability/admission, and social-emotional counseling, consultation, and program coordination. Their work includes a focus on developmental stages of student growth, including the needs, tasks, and student interests related to those stages.<ref name= "schmidt2"/>

Professional school counselors meet the needs of students in four basic domains: academic development, [[career development]] and college access/affordability/admission, and social-emotional development (Dahir & Campbell, 1997; Hatch & Bowers, 2003, 2005; ASCA, 2012).<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Carey |first1=John |last2=Dimmitt |first2=Carey |last3=Hatch |first3=Trish |last4=Lapan |first4=Richard |last5=Whiston |first5=Susan |display-authors= 3| date=February 2008 |title=Report of the National Panel for Evidence-Based School Counseling: Outcome Research Coding Protocol and Evaluation of Student Success Skills and Second Step  |journal=Professional School Counseling |volume=11 |issue=3 |pages=197–206 |doi=10.1177/2156759x0801100306 |issn= 1096-2409}}</ref>  Knowledge, understanding and skill in these domains are developed through [[classroom]] [[Teaching|instruction]], [[Educational assessment|appraisal]], consultation, [[Psychotherapy|counseling]], coordination, and [[collaboration]].  For example, in appraisal, school counselors may use a variety of [[Personality tests|personality]] and career assessment methods (such as the Self-Directed Search [SDS]<ref>{{Cite web |title=What is the SDS? {{!}} Self-Directed Search |url=http://self-directed-search.com/what-is-it/ |access-date=2021-08-19 |website=SDS Development |language=en-US}}</ref> or Career Key<ref>{{Cite web |title=About Us |url=https://www.careerkey.org/about-us |access-date= 2021-08-19 |website=CareerKey.org |language=en-US}}</ref> [based on the [[Holland Codes]]]) to help students explore career and college needs and interests.

Schools play a key role in assessment, access to services, and possible referral to appropriate outside support systems.<ref name= "Keats, P.A., & Laitsh, D.-2010">{{Cite journal |last1=Keats| first1= P.A.| last2= Laitsh| first2= D. |date=2010 |title=Contemplating Regulation of Counsellors in Canadian Schools: Current Issues and Concerns |journal=Canadian Journal of Educational Administration and Policy |volume=108}}</ref> They provide intervention, prevention, and services to support students' academic, career, and post-secondary education as well as social-emotional growth. The role of school counselors is expansive.  School  counselors address mental health issues, crisis intervention, and advising for course selection.<ref name= "Walker-2015">{{Cite journal |last=Walker |first=K. |date=2015 |title=Improving the Effectiveness of School Counselling: Consensus, Collaboration, and Clinical Supervision |journal=Canadian Journal of Counselling & Psychotherapy |volume=49 |issue=3 |pages=275–295}}</ref> School counselors consult with all stakeholders to support student needs and may also focus on [[experiential learning]], [[cooperative education]], internships, career shadowing, and entrance to specialized high school programs.<ref name="People for Education-2018">{{Cite web |date=2018 |title=Guidance counsellors: Expanding roles limited access |url=https://peopleforeducation.ca/report/guidance-report-2018/#pfe2016 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210615114525/https://peopleforeducation.ca/report/guidance-report-2018/ |archive-date=2021-06-15 |website= peopleforeducation.ca | publisher= People for Education}}</ref>

=== Methods ===
The four main school counseling program interventions include school counseling curriculum classroom lessons and annual academic, career/college access/affordability/admission, and social-emotional planning for every student; and group and individual counseling for some students.<ref>Chen-Hayes, Ockerman, & Mason, 2014</ref>

School counselor interventions include individual and group counseling for some students. For example, if a student's behavior is interfering with his or her achievement, the school counselor may observe that student in a class, provide consultation to teachers and other stakeholders to develop (with the student) a plan to address the behavioral {{Not a typo|issue(s)}}, and then collaborate to implement and evaluate the plan. They also provide consultation services to family members such as college access/affordability/admission, career development, parenting skills, [[study skills]], child and adolescent development, mental health issues, and help with school-home transitions.

School counselor interventions for all students include annual academic/career/college access/affordability/admission planning [[K–12]] and leading classroom developmental lessons on academic, career/college, and social-emotional topics.  The topics of mental health, multiculturalism (Portman, 2009), [[anti-racism]], and school safety are important areas of focus for school counselors. Often school counselors will coordinate outside groups to help with student needs such as academics, or coordinate a program that teaches about [[child abuse]] or [[Psychoactive drug|drugs]], through on-stage [[drama]].<ref name="schmidt2">{{cite book| last= Schmidt| first=J.J. | year= 2003| title= Counseling in Schools: Essential Services and Comprehensive Programs| edition=4th| place= Boston| publisher=Allyn & Bacon| isbn= | page= }}</ref>

School counselors develop, implement, and evaluate school counseling programs that deliver academic, career, college access/affordability/admission, and social-emotional competencies to all students in their schools. For example, the ASCA National Model<ref name="Hatch">{{cite book| last1=Hatch| first1= T.| last2= Bowers| first2= J. | year= 2003| edition= 2012| title=The ASCA National Model: A framework for school counseling programs| place= Alexandria, Virginia | publisher= American School Counselor Association| isbn=}}</ref> includes the following four main areas:<ref>{{cite web |title= ASCA National Model: A Framework For School Counseling Programs| website= schoolcounselor.org |url=https://www.schoolcounselor.org/asca/media/asca/ASCA%20National%20Model%20Templates/ANMExecSumm.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180322081820/https://www.schoolcounselor.org/asca/media/asca/ASCA%20National%20Model%20Templates/ANMExecSumm.pdf |archive-date= 2018-03-22 |access-date= 2018-03-21}}</ref>

* Foundation (Define as of 2019) – a school counseling program mission statement, a vision statement, a beliefs statement, SMART Goals; ASCA Mindsets & Behaviors & ASCA Code of Ethics;
* Delivery System (Deliver as of 2019) – how school counseling core curriculum lessons, planning for every student, and individual and group counseling are delivered in direct and indirect services to students (80% of school counselor time);
* Management System (Manage as of 2019) – calendars; use of data tool; use of time tool; administrator-school counselor agreement; school counseling program advisory council; small group, school counseling core curriculum, and closing the gap action plans; and
* Accountability System (Assess as of 2019) – school counseling program assessment; small group, school counseling core curriculum, and closing-the-gap results reports; and school counselor performance evaluations based on school counselor competencies.<ref>{{Cite web |date= 2020-12-18 |title=ASCA National Model| website= schoolcounselor.org |publisher= American School Counselor Association |url=https://www.schoolcounselor.org/school-counselors-members/asca-national-model |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201218053849/https://www.schoolcounselor.org/school-counselors-members/asca-national-model |archive-date=2020-12-18 |access-date= 2021-08-19}}</ref>

The school counseling program model (ASCA, 2012, 2019) is implemented using key skills from the National Center for Transforming School Counseling's Transforming School Counseling Initiative:  Advocacy, Leadership, Teaming and Collaboration, and Systemic Change.

School Counselors are expected to follow a professional code of ethics in many countries. For example, In the US, they are the American School Counselor Association (ASCA) School Counselor Ethical Code, the [[American Counseling Association]] (ACA) Code of Ethics, and the [[National Association for College Admission Counseling]] (NACAC) Statement of Principles of Good Practice (SPGP).<ref>{{Cite web |date= 2012-02-17 |title=Statement of Principles of Good Practice |url=http://www.nacacnet.org/ABOUTNACAC/POLICIES/Documents/SPGP.pdf| website= nacacnet.org |url-status=live |publisher=National Association for College Admission Counseling |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120217003936/http://www.nacacnet.org/ABOUTNACAC/POLICIES/Documents/SPGP.pdf |archive-date= 2012-02-17 |access-date=2021-08-19}} In the UK, they refer to the [https://www.ispc.org.uk/ International Society of Psychotherapy and Counselling] (ISPC) and the NCS for ethics and guidance.</ref>

=== Role confusion ===
Some school counselors experience role confusion, given the many tasks they are expected to perform.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1= Chandler| first1= J. W. |last2= Burnham | first2= J. J. |last3= Riechel | first3= M. E. K. |last4= Dahir | first4= C. A. |last5= Stone | first5= C. B. |last6= Oliver | first6= D. F. | last7= Bledsoe | first7= K. G. |display-authors= 3| year=2018 |title=Assessing the counseling and non-counseling roles of school counselors |journal=Journal of School Counseling |volume=16 |issue=7}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Beningo |first=S. |date=2017 |title=Counselor perceptions: Let us do our job! |journal=Journal of Education and Learning |volume=6 |issue=4 |pages= 175–180 |doi=10.5539/jel.v6n4p175 |doi-access=free}}</ref><ref name= "Walker-2015"/><ref>{{cite web |title=Role of the guidance teacher-counsellor |url=http://osca.ca/docs/OSCA-English-Final-Web.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210729084215/http://osca.ca/docs/OSCA-English-Final-Web.pdf |archive-date= 2021-07-29 |publisher=Ontario School Counsellors Association}}</ref><ref name= "People for Education-2018"/><ref name="Keats, P.A., & Laitsh, D.-2010" /> The demands on the school counselor to be a generalist who performs roles in leadership, advocacy, essential services, and curriculum development can be too much if there is not a clear mission, vision, and [[comprehensive school]] counseling program in place.<ref name="Keats, P.A., & Laitsh, D.-2010" /> Additionally, some school counselors are stretched too thin to provide mental health support on top of their other duties.<ref name= "Walker-2015" />

The role of a school counselor is critical and needs to be supported by all stakeholders to ensure equity and access for all students, particularly those with the fewest resources.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Deslonde |first=Vernell |date=December 5, 2018 |title=!High School Counselors' Influence on Low Socioeconomic Students' College Enrollment |journal=Journal of School Counseling |volume= 24 |pages=1–29}}</ref> The roles of school counselors are expanding<ref>{{Cite journal |last= Pascopella |first=Angela |date=July 2004 |title= Positive Power. |journal=District Administration |volume=40}}</ref> and changing with time<ref>{{Cite journal |last= Sackett |first=Corrine R. |date=October 2018 |title=A Phenomenological Inquiry of High School Students' Meaningful Experiences with School Counselors. |journal=Journal of School Counseling |volume=16 |pages=1–31}}</ref> As roles change, school counselors help students prosper in academics, career, post-secondary, and social-emotional domains. School counselors reduce and bridge the inequalities facing students in educational systems.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Robinson |first1=Karen Jeong |last2=Roksa |first2=Josipa |date=2016-01-18 |title=Counselors, Information, and High School College-Going Culture: Inequalities in the College Application Process |journal= Research in Higher Education |volume=57 |issue=7 |pages=845–868 |doi=10.1007/s11162-016-9406-2 |issn=0361-0365 |s2cid=147579390}}</ref>

== Types of school counselors ==
{{Worldwide|date=July 2024|2=US|section|reason=Additional information is needed to review the necessary requirements for other countries.}}

=== Elementary school counselor ===
[[Elementary school]] counselors provide<ref name="Stone">Stone, C. B., & Dahir, C. A. (2006). ''The Transformed School Counselor''. Boston, MA: Lahaska Press/Houghton Mifflin.</ref> academic, career, college access, and personal and social competencies and planning to all students, and individual and group counseling for some students and their families to meet the developmental needs of young children [[primary school|K–6]].<ref name="Hatch & Bowers, 2003; ASCA, 20122">(Hatch & Bowers, 2003; ASCA, 2012)</ref> Transitions from pre-school to elementary school and from elementary school to middle school are an important focus for elementary school counselors. Increased emphasis is placed on accountability for helping close achievement and opportunity gaps at the elementary level as more school counseling programs move to evidence-based work with data and specific results.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Young |first1=Anita |title=Making DATA work |last2=Kaffenberger |first2=Carol |year=2009 |publisher=American School Counselor Association |isbn=978-1-929289-55-4 |oclc=1157317058}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Dimmitt |first1=Carey |title=Evidence-based school counseling : making a difference with data-driven practices |last2=Carey |first2=John C. |last3=Hatch |first3=Trish |date=2007 |isbn=978-1-4522-0760-5 |location=Thousand Oaks, CA |oclc=907678474}}</ref>

School counseling programs that deliver specific competencies to all students help to close achievement and opportunity gaps.<ref name="Bryan, Holcomb-McCoy 20072">(Bryan, Holcomb-McCoy, Moore-Thomas, & Day-Vines, 2009; College Board, 2008; Holcomb-McCoy, 2007)</ref> To facilitate individual and group school counseling interventions, school counselors use developmental, cognitive-behavioral, person-centered (Rogerian) listening and influencing skills, systemic, family, multicultural,<ref name="Holcomb-McCoy 20092">(Holcomb-McCoy & Chen-Hayes, 2007; Portman, 2009)</ref> narrative, and play therapy theories and techniques.<ref name="Stone"/><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Shen |first1=Yih-Jiun |last2=Herr |first2=Edwin L. |date=2003-03-01 |title=Perceptions of Play Therapy in Taiwan: The Voices of School Counselors and Counselor Educators |journal=International Journal for the Advancement of Counselling |language=en |volume=25 |issue=1 |pages=27–41 |doi=10.1023/A:1024924111194 |issn=1573-3246 |s2cid=140433782|doi-access=free }}</ref> Sink & Stroh (2003) released a research study showing the effectiveness of elementary school counseling programs in Washington state.<ref>Sink, C. A.; Stroh, H. R. (2003). "Raising achievement test scores of early elementary school students through comprehensive school counseling programs". ''Professional School Counseling''. '''6''': 352–364.</ref>

=== Middle school counselor ===
[[Middle school]] counselors provide school counseling curriculum lessons<ref name="Stone"/> on academic, career, college access, and personal and social competencies, advising and academic/career/college access planning to all students and individual and group counseling for some students and their families to meet the needs of older children/early adolescents in grades 7 and 8.<ref>Hatch, T., & Bowers, J. (2003, 2005, 2012). ''The ASCA National Model: A framework for school counseling programs.'' Alexandria, VA: American School Counselor Association.</ref>

Middle School College Access curricula have been developed to assist students and their families before reaching high school. To facilitate the school counseling process, school counselors use theories and techniques including developmental, cognitive-behavioral, person-centered (Rogerian) listening and influencing skills, systemic, family, multicultural,<ref name="Holcomb-McCoy 20092">(Holcomb-McCoy & Chen-Hayes, 2007; Portman, 2009)</ref> narrative, and play therapy.  Transitional issues to ensure successful transitions to high school are a key area including career exploration and assessment with seventh and eighth grade students.<ref name="schmidt3">Schmidt, J.J. (2003) ''Counseling in schools: Essential services and comprehensive programs''. 4th ed. Boston: Allyn & Bacon.</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Trolley |first1=Barbara |title=The school counselor's guide to special education |last2=Haas |first2=Heather |last3=Patti |first3=Danielle Campese |date=2009 |publisher=Corwin Press |isbn=978-1-4416-5260-7 |location=Thousand Oaks, Calif. |oclc=639015889}}</ref> Sink, Akos, Turnbull, & Mvududu released a study in 2008 confirming the effectiveness of middle school comprehensive school counseling programs in Washington state.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Sink |first1=Christopher |last2=Akos |first2=Patrick |last3=Turnbull |first3=Rebecca |last4=Mvududu |first4=Nyaradzo |date=October 2008 |title=An Investigation of Comprehensive School Counseling Programs and Academic Achievement in Washington State Middle Schools |journal=Professional School Counseling |volume=12 |issue=1 |pages=43–53 |doi= 10.1177/2156759X0801200105|issn=1096-2409}}</ref>

=== High school counselor ===
[[High school]] counselors provide<ref name="Stone"/> academic, career, college access, and personal and social competencies with developmental classroom lessons and planning to all students, and individual and group counseling for some students and their families to meet the developmental needs of adolescents (Hatch & Bowers, 2003, 2005, 2012).<ref name="ReferenceA2">Hatch & Bowers, 2003; ASCA, 2012</ref> Emphasis is on college access counseling at the early high school level as more school counseling programs move to evidence-based work with data and specific results<ref name="Young 20072">(Young & Kaffenberger, 2009; Dimmitt, Carey, & Hatch, 2007)</ref> that show how school counseling programs help to close achievement, opportunity, and attainment gaps ensuring all students have access to school counseling programs and early college access/affordability/admission activities.<ref>(ASCA, 2010; Bryan, Holcomb-McCoy, Moore-Thomas, & Day-Vines, 2009; Carey et al., 2008; Holcomb-McCoy, 2007)</ref> The breadth of demands high school counselors face, from educational attainment (high school graduation and some students' preparation for careers and college) to student social and mental health, has led to ambiguous role definition.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Paisley |first1=P |year=2001 |title=School Counseling for the 21st Century: Challenges and Opportunities |journal=Professional School Counseling |volume=5 |issue=2 |page=106}}</ref> Summarizing a 2011 national survey of more than 5,330 middle school and high school counselors, researchers argued: "Despite the aspirations of counselors to effectively help students succeed in school and fulfill their dreams, the mission and roles of counselors in the education system must be more clearly defined; schools must create measures of accountability to track their effectiveness; and policymakers and key stakeholders must integrate counselors into reform efforts to maximize their impact in schools across America".<ref>Bridgeland, J. and Bruce, M. (2011). 2011 National Survey of School Counselors: Counseling at a Crossroads. The College Board.</ref>

Transitional issues to ensure successful transitions to college, other post-secondary educational options, and careers are a key area.<ref>{{cite web |date=19 August 2014 |title=Advocacy |url=http://professionals.collegeboard.com/policy-advocacy/educators/nosca |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100325213654/http://professionals.collegeboard.com/policy-advocacy/educators/nosca |archive-date=25 March 2010 |access-date=9 May 2018 |website=collegeboard.com}}</ref> The high school counselor helps students and their families prepare for post-secondary education including college and careers (e.g. [[college]], [[careers]]) by engaging students and their families in accessing and evaluating accurate information on what the National Office for School Counselor Advocacy calls the eight essential elements of college and career counseling: (1) College Aspirations, (2) Academic Planning for Career and College Readiness, (3) Enrichment and Extracurricular Engagement, (4) College and Career Exploration and Selection Processes, (5) College and Career Assessments, (6) College Affordability Planning, (7) College and Career Admission Processes, and (8) Transition from High School Graduation to College Enrollment.<ref>{{cite web |title=NOSCA Tools & Resources - College Board - Advocacy &#124; NOSCA: National Office for School Counselor Advocacy |url=http://advocacy.collegeboard.org/college-preparation-access/national-office-school-counselor-advocacy-nosca/news/8-components-college |url-status=live |archive-url=http://archive.wikiwix.com/cache/20110812162927/http://advocacy.collegeboard.org/college-preparation-access/national-office-school-counselor-advocacy-nosca/news/8-components-college |archive-date=2011-08-12 |access-date=2012-11-07 |publisher=Advocacy.collegeboard.org}}</ref> Some students turn to private college admissions advisors but there is no research evidence that private college admissions advisors have any effectiveness in assisting students attain selective college admissions.

Lapan, Gysbers & Sun showed correlational evidence of the effectiveness of fully implemented school counseling programs on high school students' academic success.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Lapan |first1=Richard T. |last2=Gysbers |first2=Norman C. |last3=Sun |first3=Yongmin |date=1997 |title=The Impact of More Fully Implemented Guidance Programs on the School Experiences of High School Students: A Statewide Evaluation Study |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1002/j.1556-6676.1997.tb02344.x |journal=Journal of Counseling & Development |language=en |volume=75 |issue=4 |pages=292–302 |doi=10.1002/j.1556-6676.1997.tb02344.x |issn=1556-6676|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Carey et al.'s 2008 study showed specific best practices from high school counselors raising college-going rates within a strong college-going environment in multiple USA-based high schools with large numbers of students of non-dominant cultural identities.[[File:Educator eff.jpg|thumb|Some school counselors use bibliotherapy, i.e., books and other media, to help students in individual and group counseling and classroom counseling lessons.]]

==Global standing of school counselors==
{| class="wikitable"
|+Countries that provide school counseling<ref>{{Cite web |title=ISCA - Home |url=https://iscainfo.com/ |access-date=2023-07-06 |website=iscainfo.com}}</ref>
|Armenia
|Australia
|Austria
|Bahamas
|Belgium

|Botswana

|Estonia

|Gambia

|Italy

|Lebanon

|Oman
|Philippines
|Poland
|Portugal

|-
|Rwanda
|Saudi Arabia
|Serbia
|Singapore
|Slovakia
|-
|South Korea
|Spain
|St. Kitts
|Sweden
|Switzerland

|Syria
|Taiwan

|-
|Turkey
|Uganda
|United Arab Emirates
|United Kingdom
|United States

|Venezuela
|Vietnam

|
|
|}
A school counselor is an integral part of the education system in countries representing over half of the world's population and in other countries it is emerging as a critical support for elementary, middle, and high school learning, as well as post-secondary options.<ref name="counsellingminded.com2">Dr. Belinda Harris, International school-based counselling scoping report {{cite web |title=Error &#124; BACP |url=https://www.bacp.co.uk/media/2050/counselling-minded-international-school-based-counselling-harris.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180214150240/https://www.bacp.co.uk/media/2050/counselling-minded-international-school-based-counselling-harris.pdf |archive-date=2018-02-14 |access-date=2018-02-14}}</ref>

Countries vary in how a school counseling program and services are provided based on economics (funding for schools and school counseling programs), social capital ([[Private schools in the United Kingdom|private]] versus public schools), and school counselor certification and credentialing movements in education departments, professional associations, and local, state/province, and national legislation.<ref name="iaevg.org3">http://www.iaevg.org {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141217141109/http://iaevg.org/|date=2014-12-17}}; www.vanguardofcounsellors.org; Dr. Belinda Harris, International school-based counselling scoping report {{cite web |title=Error &#124; BACP |url=https://www.bacp.co.uk/media/2050/counselling-minded-international-school-based-counselling-harris.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180214150240/https://www.bacp.co.uk/media/2050/counselling-minded-international-school-based-counselling-harris.pdf |archive-date=2018-02-14 |access-date=2018-02-14}}</ref>

School counseling is established in 62 countries and emerging in another seven.<ref name="counsellingminded.com2"/>  An international scoping project on school-based counseling showed school counseling is mandatory in 39 countries, 32 USA states, one Australian state, three German states, two countries in the United Kingdom, and three provinces in Canada.<ref name="counsellingminded.com2"/> Some countries within the Americas, Africa, Asia, Europe, and the Pacific Islands lack formal school counseling programs and instead utilize teachers or psychologists to fulfill the school counselor position with an emphasis on career development.<ref name="iaevg.org3"/> Moreover, In some countries, school counseling is provided by school counseling specialists (for example, Botswana, China, Finland, Israel, Malta, Nigeria, Romania, Taiwan, Turkey, United States). In other cases, school counseling is provided by classroom teachers who either have such duties added to their typical teaching load or teach only a limited load that also includes school counseling activities (India, Japan, Mexico, South Korea, Zambia).<ref name="iaevg.org22">http://www.iaevg.org {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141217141109/http://iaevg.org/|date=2014-12-17}}; www.vanguardofcounsellors.org; Dr. Belinda Harris, International school-based counselling scoping report {{cite web |title=Error &#124; BACP |url=https://www.bacp.co.uk/media/2050/counselling-minded-international-school-based-counselling-harris.pdf |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180214150240/https://www.bacp.co.uk/media/2050/counselling-minded-international-school-based-counselling-harris.pdf |archive-date=2018-02-14 |access-date=2018-02-14}}</ref> The IAEVG focuses on career development with some international school counseling articles and conference presentations.<ref name="iaevg.org22"/> Both the IAEVG and the Vanguard of Counsellors promote school counseling internationally.

=== Canada ===
The roots of school counseling stemmed from a response to the conditions created by the industrial revolution in the early 1900s.<ref name="Pelech-2006">{{Cite journal |last=Pelech |first=Sharon |date=2006 |title=The Role of Guidance Counsellors in Calgary's Career Pathways Initiative |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.18848/1447-9494/cgp/v12i06/47902 |journal=The International Journal of Learning: Annual Review |volume=12 |issue=6 |pages=351–358 |doi=10.18848/1447-9494/cgp/v12i06/47902 |issn=1447-9494|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Originally, school counseling was often referred to as vocational guidance, where the goal of the profession was to help individuals find their path in a time where individuals previous ways of making a living had been displaced.<ref name="Pelech-2006" /> As people moved towards industrialized cities, counseling was required to help students navigate these new vocations. With a great discrepancy between the rich and the poor, vocational counseling was initiated to help support disadvantaged students.<ref name="Pelech-2006" /> After [[World War II]], vocational guidance began to shift towards a new movement of counseling, which provided a theoretical backing.<ref name="Pelech-2006" /> As the role of school counselors progressed into the 1960s, 1970s and 1980s there has become more uncertainty as to what the role entails.<ref name="Pelech-2006" /> This role confusion continues into the 21st century, where there is a lack of clear consensus between counselors, other teachers, administration, students and parents on what school counselors should be prioritizing.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Nadon |first1=Daniel |last2=Samson |first2=André |last3=Gazzola |first3=Nicola |last4=Thériault |first4=Anne |date=2015-10-23 |title=Becoming a guidance counsellor in Ontario: formative influences from counsellors' perspective |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1007/s10775-015-9317-3 |journal=International Journal for Educational and Vocational Guidance |volume=16 |issue=3 |pages=363–377 |doi=10.1007/s10775-015-9317-3 |s2cid=254572783 |issn=1873-0388|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

=== China ===
China has put substantial financial resources into school counseling with strong growth in urban areas but less than 1% of rural students receive it; China does not mandate school counseling.<ref name="Thomason-2008">{{Cite journal |last1=Thomason |first1=Timothy C. |last2=Qiong |first2=Xiao |date=2008-07-26 |title=Counseling Psychology in China: Past and Present |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1007/s10447-008-9056-y |journal=International Journal for the Advancement of Counselling |volume=30 |issue=4 |pages=213–219 |doi=10.1007/s10447-008-9056-y |s2cid=54685699 |issn=0165-0653|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

In China, Thomason & Qiong discussed the main influences on school counseling as Chinese philosophers Confucius and Lao-Tzu, who provided early models of child and adult development who influenced the work of [[Abraham Maslow]] and Carl Rogers.<ref name="Thomason-2008" />

Only 15% of high school students are admitted to college in China, so entrance exams are fiercely competitive. Students entering university graduate at a rate of 99%.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Chang |first=Liu |date=2019 |title=Assisting College Students' Mental Health Education in Mainland ——Research on the Psychological Consultation Model of Hong Kong, Taiwan and Chinese Mainland |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.35534/tppc.0110035 |journal=Theory and Practice of Psychological Counseling |volume=1 |issue=10 |pages=509–526 |doi=10.35534/tppc.0110035 |s2cid=213564401 |issn=2664-1828|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Much pressure is put on children and adolescents to study and attend college. This pressure is a central focus of school counseling in China. An additional stressor is that there are not enough places for students to attend college, and over one-third of college graduates cannot find jobs,[19] so career and employment counseling and development are also central in school counseling.

In China, there is a stigma related to social-emotional and mental health issues; therefore, even though most universities and many (urban) primary and secondary schools have school counselors, many students are reluctant to seek counseling for issues such as anxiety and depression. There is no national system of certifying school counselors. Most are trained in Western-developed cognitive methods including REBT, Rogerian, Family Systems, Behavior Modification, and Object Relations.<ref name="Thomason-2008" /> School counselors also recommend Chinese methods such as qigong (deep breathing) and acupuncture, as well as music therapy.<ref name="Thomason-2008" /><ref>{{Cite book |last=Jiang |first=Zhiyong |title=SEG Technical Program Expanded Abstracts 2006 |chapter=Migration of interbed multiple reflections |date=January 2006   |chapter-url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1190/1.2370263 |pages=3501–3505 |publisher=Society of Exploration Geophysicists |doi=10.1190/1.2370263|chapter-url-access=subscription }}</ref> Chinese school counselors work within a traditional Chinese worldview of a community and family-based system that lessens the focus on the individual. In Hong Kong, Hui (2000) discussed work moving toward comprehensive school counseling programs and eliminating the older remediation-style model.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Hui |first=Eadaoin K.P. |date=2000 |title=Guidance as a whole school approach in Hong Kong: From remediation to student development |url=http://link.springer.com/10.1023/A:1005494502922 |journal=International Journal for the Advancement of Counselling |volume=22 |issue=1 |pages=69–82 |doi=10.1023/A:1005494502922|s2cid=115142593 |url-access=subscription }}</ref>

Middle school students are a priority for school counseling services in China.

==School counseling associations==
School counselors around the world are affiliated with various national and regional school counseling associations, and abide by their guidelines. These associations include:

* African Counseling Association (AfCA)
* {{Lang|es|Asociacion Argentina de Counselors|italic=no}} (AAC-Argentina)
* {{Lang|pt|Associacao Portuguesa de Psicoterapia centrada na Pessoa e de Counselling|italic=no}} (APPCPC-Portugal)
* Australian Guidance and Counselling Association (AGCA)
* Hong Kong Association of Guidance Masters and Career Masters (HKAGMCM)
* Cypriot Association of School Guidance Counsellors (OELMEK)
* European Counseling Association (ECA)
* France Ministry of Education
* Hellenic Society of Counselling and Guidance (HESCOG-Greece)
* International Baccalaureate (IB)
* International Society of Psychotherapy and Counselling (ISPC)
* International Vanguard of Counsellors (IVC)
* International Association for Educational and Vocational Guidance (IAEVG)
* {{Lang|fr|Association Internationale d'Orientation Scolaire et Professionnelle|italic=no}} (AIOSP)
* {{Lang|de|Internationale Vereinigung für Schul- und Berufsberatung|italic=no}} (IVSBB)
* {{Lang|es|Asociación Internacional para la Orientación Educativa y Profesional|italic=no}} (AIOEP)
* Institute of Guidance Counselors (IGC) (Ireland)
* Kenya Association of Professional Counselors (KAPC)
* Department of Education-Malta
* New Zealand Association of Counsellors/{{Lang|mi|Te Roopu Kaiwhiriwhiri o Aotearoa|italic=no}} (NZAC)
* Counseling Association of Nigeria (CASSON)
* Philippine Guidance and Counseling Association (PGCA)
* Counseling & Psychotherapy in Scotland (COSCA)
* Singapore Association for Counseling (SAC)
* {{Lang|es|Federación Española de Orientación y Psicopedagogía|italic=no}} (FEOP-Spain)
* The Taiwan Guidance and Counseling Association (TGCA)
* Counselling Children and Young People (BACP affiliate, UK)
* British Association for Counselling and Psychotherapy (BACP-UK)
* American Counseling Association (ACA-USA)
* American School Counselor Association (ASCA-USA)
* Center for Excellence in School Counseling and Leadership (CESCaL) (USA)
* Center for School Counseling Outcome Research (CSCOR-USA) Council for the Accreditation of Counseling and Related Educational Programs (CACREP-USA and international)
* National Board for Certified Counselors (NBCC, USA)
* National Office for School Counselor Advocacy (NOSCA) at The College Board (USA)
* National Center for Transforming School Counseling (NCTSC) at The Education Trust (USA)
* Overseas Association of College Admissions Counselors (OACAC an affiliate of National Association of College Admissions Counselors-USA)
*Canadian Counselling and Psychotherapy Association – National School Counsellors Chapter (CPPA)<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.ccpa-accp.ca/chapters/school-counsellors/|title=National association: School Counsellors Chapter|date=2019|publisher=Canadian Counselling and Psychotherapy Association}}</ref>
*Newfoundland and Labrador Counsellors' and Psychologists' Association<ref name="Canadian Counselling and Psychotherapy Association-2019">{{Cite web|date=2019|title=Provincial and Territorial Associations for School Counsellors|url=https://www.ccpa-accp.ca/chapters/school-counsellors/#id15|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210308121145/https://www.ccpa-accp.ca/chapters/school-counsellors/|archive-date=2021-03-08|publisher=Canadian Counselling and Psychotherapy Association}}</ref>
*PEI Counselling Association<ref name="Canadian Counselling and Psychotherapy Association-2019" />
*British Columbia School Counsellors<ref name="Canadian Counselling and Psychotherapy Association-2019" />
*Guidance Council of the Alberta Teachers' Association<ref name="Canadian Counselling and Psychotherapy Association-2019" />
*Ontario School Counsellors' Association<ref name="Canadian Counselling and Psychotherapy Association-2019" />
*Nova Scotia School Counsellors Association<ref name="Canadian Counselling and Psychotherapy Association-2019" />

== Education, certification, training, & accreditation ==
{{Worldwide|date=July 2024|2=US|section|reason=Additional information is needed to review the necessary requirements for other countries.}}
In the United States, a master's degree in counseling, school counseling, or related fields is required to become a school counselor. A few states may require additional training and/or certification to be eligible for employment. However, all public school counselors must receive a state government issued credential in order to practice counseling.<ref name="www.bls.gov" /> Obtaining  certification/licensure requires a master's degree, an internship and/or practicum experience with a licensed school counselor, and completion of either a state or national test.<ref>{{Cite web |title=State Requirements & Programs - American School Counselor Association (ASCA) |url=https://www.schoolcounselor.org/About-School-Counseling/State-Requirements-Programs |access-date=2023-06-23 |website=www.schoolcounselor.org}}</ref> A few states may require counselors to have classroom teaching experience prior to becoming a school counselor.<ref name="www.bls.gov" />

Those wanting to pursue a career in school counseling should ensure that their university program maintains the appropriate accreditation. In the United States, the largest accreditation body for Counselor Education/School Counseling programs is [[The Council for Accreditation of Counseling and Related Educational Programs|the Council for the Accreditation of Counseling and Related Educational Programs]] (CACREP).<ref>{{cite web |date=2012-10-17 |title=Council for Accreditation of Counseling and Related Educational Programs - Welcome to CACREP |url=http://www.cacrep.org |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140403054231/http://www.cacrep.org/ |archive-date=2014-04-03 |access-date=2012-11-07 |publisher=Cacrep.org}}</ref> International Counselor Education programs are accredited through a CACREP affiliate, the International Registry of Counselor Education Programs (IRCEP).

The education of school counselors around the world varies based on the laws and cultures of countries and the historical influences of their educational and credentialing systems and professional identities related to who delivers academic, career, college readiness, and personal/social information, advising, curriculum, and counseling and related services.<ref>{{Citation |last=Harris |first=Belinda |title=Using Scoping Reviews to Document and Synthesize Current Practices in School-Based Counselling |date=2017 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1007/978-3-319-58179-8_4 |work=International Handbook for Policy Research on School-Based Counseling |pages=45–57 |access-date=2023-06-24 |place=Cham |publisher=Springer International Publishing |doi=10.1007/978-3-319-58179-8_4 |isbn=978-3-319-58177-4|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

== Salary, benefits, and expected job growth ==
{{Worldwide|date=July 2024|2=US|section|reason=Additional information is needed to report on worldwide salary, benefits, and expected job growth statistics.}}
In 2021, the median pay for school counselors in the United States was $60,510 annually or $29.09 per hour. But, a school counselor's salary can range anywhere between $37,550 to $98,190 according the most recent report completed by the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics.<ref name="www.bls.gov">{{Cite web |title=School and Career Counselors and Advisors : Occupational Outlook Handbook: : U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics |url=https://www.bls.gov/ooh/community-and-social-service/school-and-career-counselors.htm |access-date=2023-06-23 |website=www.bls.gov |language=en-us}}</ref>

Most State Education Boards require that school districts offer school counselors health, dental, and vision insurance. Additionally, school counselors can receive retirement plans as well as professional development assistance.<ref>{{Cite web  |title=Benefits & Plans |url=https://ogi.idaho.gov/benefits-plans/ |access-date=2023-06-23 |website=Office of Group Insurance |language=EN}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=School Employees Benefits Board (SEBB) Program {{!}} Washington State Health Care Authority |url=https://www.hca.wa.gov/about-hca/programs-and-initiatives/school-employees-benefits-board-sebb-program |access-date=2023-06-23 |website=www.hca.wa.gov}}</ref> Lastly, school counselors enjoy the same vacation time as students, meaning counselors receive time off for each holiday that falls within the school calendar as well as a summer vacation each year.

The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics projects the school counselor position to grow 10% between 2021 and 2031; meaning there will be an additional 32,000 job openings within the next 10 years.<ref name="www.bls.gov" />

====Academic interventions, closing achievement gaps====

*{{cite journal | last1 = Blanco | first1 = P. J. | last2 = Ray | first2 = D. C. | year = 2006 | title = Evaluating the impact of an innovative approach to integrate conflict resolution into the academic curriculum on middle school students' academic achievement | journal = Professional School Counseling | volume = 9 | pages = 190–196 | doi = 10.1177/2156759X0500900309 }}
*{{cite journal | last1 = Schellenberg | first1 = R. | last2 = Grothaus | first2 = T. | year = 2011 | title = Using culturally competent responsive services to improve student achievement and behavior | journal = Professional School Counseling | volume = 14 | issue = 3 | pages = 222–230 | doi= 10.1177/2156759X1101400306}}

*Schellenberg, R. (2008). The new school counselor: Strategies for universal academic achievement. Lanham, MD: Rowman Littlefield Education.
*{{cite journal | last1 = Sciarra | first1 = D. T. | year = 2010 | title = Predictive factors in intensive math course-taking in high school | journal = Professional School Counseling | volume = 13 | issue = 3| pages = 196–207 | doi= 10.1177/2156759X1001300307}}
*Squier, K. L., Nailor, P., & Carey, J. C. (2014). ''Achieving excellence in school counseling through motivation, self-direction, self-knowledge, and relationships.'' Thousand Oaks, CA: Corwin.
*{{cite journal | last1 = Suh | first1 = S. | last2 = Suh | first2 = J. | last3 = Houston | first3 = I. | year = 2007 | title = Predictors of categorical at–risk high school dropouts | journal = Journal of Counseling and Development | volume = 85 | issue = 2| pages = 196–203 | doi=10.1002/j.1556-6678.2007.tb00463.x}}
*{{cite journal | last1 = Suh | first1 = S. | last2 = Suh | first2 = J. | year = 2007 | title = Risk factors and levels of risk for high school dropouts | journal = Professional School Counseling | volume = 10 | issue = 3| pages = 297–306 | doi=10.1177/2156759X0701000312}}
